words.g: Adam the name of the first man

verses.flow: Adam, Seth, Enosh;
verses.note: STRUCTURE: The Chronicler opens with an unadorned genealogical list beginning with the first man, Adam, without narrative introduction. This literary device, called a toledot (generations/genealogy, Strong's H8435), anchors all human history — and ultimately the Davidic line — in the created order. The placement of Chronicles at the end of the Hebrew canon makes this opening a recapitulation of all preceding biblical history, from Genesis to restoration. TYPOLOGY: Beginning with Adam anticipates the NT's use of Adam-Christ typology (Rom 5:12-21; 1 Cor 15:45), where the Chronicler's genealogy ultimately culminates in the Davidic king who will be the last Adam.

verses.flow: Kenan, Mahalalel, Jared;
Enoch, Methuselah, Lamech;
Noah, Shem, Ham, and Japheth.
verses.note: HEBREW: The listing of Noah's three sons — Shem, Ham, and Japheth — follows the Table of Nations structure of Genesis 10. The Chronicler then reverses the order (Japheth first in vv. 5-7, then Ham in vv. 8-16, then Shem in vv. 17-27), saving the Semitic line for last as the line of promise. This is a deliberate rhetorical narrowing from all humanity toward the covenant people. THEOLOGICAL: The inclusion of all seventy nations (derived from the Genesis 10 table) reflects the universal scope of YHWH's sovereignty over all peoples before focusing on Israel.

verses.flow: The sons of Japheth: Gomer, Magog, Madai, Javan, Tubal, Meshech, and Tiras.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The Table of Nations lists seventy nations descending from Noah's sons. In Deuteronomy 32:8 (LXX and Dead Sea Scrolls reading), YHWH apportioned these seventy nations to the "sons of God" (divine council members), while Israel remained YHWH's own portion. This genealogy thus carries implicit divine council theology: the nations listed here are the very peoples assigned to heavenly rulers, whose authority will ultimately be reclaimed by YHWH through Israel's Messiah (Ps 82; Dan 10; Rev 5:9).

verses.flow: And the sons of Gomer: Ashkenaz, Diphath, and Togarmah.
And the sons of Javan: Elishah, Tarshish, Kittim, and Rodanim.
The sons of Ham: Cush, Mizraim, Put, and Canaan.
And the sons of Cush: Seba, Havilah, Sabta, Raama, and Sabteca. And the sons of Raama: Sheba and Dedan.
And Cush fathered Nimrod. He was the first to be a mighty one on the earth.
verses.note: HEBREW: The term "mighty one" translates gibbor (Strong's H1368), meaning a powerful warrior or champion. Nimrod is called the first gibbor on the earth, an echo of Genesis 10:8-9. His name and role may carry negative connotations as a founder of Babel/Babylon (Gen 10:10), representing human empire-building in defiance of YHWH. HISTORICAL: Nimrod is associated in ANE tradition with Mesopotamian kingship ideology; some scholars connect him with historical Assyrian or Babylonian rulers. His inclusion here reminds readers that worldly imperial power stands in contrast to YHWH's kingdom.

words.g: a Ludite or inhabitants of Lud
a Ludite or inhabitants of Lud

verses.flow: And Mizraim fathered the Ludim, the Anamim, the Lehabim, the Naphtuhim,
the Pathrusim, the Casluhim (from whom came the Philistines), and the Caphtorim.
And Canaan fathered Sidon his firstborn, and Heth,
and the Jebusite, the Amorite, the Girgashite,
the Hivite, the Arkite, the Sinite,
the Arvadite, the Zemarite, and the Hamathite.
The sons of Shem: Elam, Asshur, Arpachshad, Lud, Aram, Uz, Hul, Gether, and Meshech.
verses.note: HEBREW: The Masoretic Text here includes "Uz, Hul, Gether, and Meshech" as sons of Shem, whereas Genesis 10:23 lists these as sons of Aram (grandson of Shem). The Chronicler may be telescoping the genealogy or following a variant tradition. "Meshech" here differs from the Meshech listed among Japheth's sons (v. 5), possibly representing a distinct figure or a textual variant (some MSS read "Mash" as in Gen 10:23). THEOLOGICAL: Shem's line receives the most detailed treatment because it is the covenant line leading to Abraham, the patriarchs, and ultimately the Davidic monarchy.

verses.flow: And Arpachshad fathered Shelah, and Shelah fathered Eber.
And to Eber were born two sons: the name of the one was Peleg, for in his days the earth was divided; and the name of his brother was Joktan.
verses.note: HEBREW: The name Peleg (Strong's H6389) derives from the root palag, meaning "to divide" or "to split." The text provides an explicit etymological explanation: "for in his days the earth was divided (niphlegah)." This likely refers to the division of peoples at Babel (Gen 11:1-9), though some interpreters have proposed a reference to a geographic or tectonic division. HISTORICAL: The Babel dispersion narrative provides the narrative backdrop for the entire Table of Nations and explains the proliferation of languages and peoples across the ANE world.

verses.flow: And Joktan fathered Almodad, Sheleph, Hazarmaveth, Jerah,
Hadoram, Uzal, Diklah,
Ebal, Abimael, Sheba,
Ophir, Havilah, and Jobab. All these were sons of Joktan.
Shem, Arpachshad, Shelah;
Eber, Peleg, Reu;
Serug, Nahor, Terah;
Abram — he is Abraham.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The identification "Abram — he is Abraham" is a pivotal moment in the genealogy. The Chronicler acknowledges the covenant name-change (Gen 17:5) without narrating it, assuming reader familiarity. Abraham's name change from "exalted father" (ab-ram) to "father of a multitude" (ab-raham) encapsulates the Abrahamic covenant's promise of innumerable descendants. This verse marks the transition from universal genealogy (all nations) to the particular covenant line that will become Israel.

verses.flow: The sons of Abraham: Isaac and Ishmael.
These are their generations: the firstborn of Ishmael was Nebaioth; then Kedar, Adbeel, Mibsam,
Mishma, Dumah, Massa, Hadad, Tema,
Jetur, Naphish, and Kedemah. These are the sons of Ishmael.
And the sons of Keturah, the concubine of Abraham: she bore Zimran, Jokshan, Medan, Midian, Ishbak, and Shuah. And the sons of Jokshan: Sheba and Dedan.
And the sons of Midian: Ephah, Epher, Hanoch, Abida, and Eldaah. All these were sons of Keturah.

verses.flow: And Abraham fathered Isaac. The sons of Isaac: Esau and Israel.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The pairing "Esau and Israel" is theologically loaded. By using "Israel" rather than "Jacob," the Chronicler employs the covenant name, signaling that Jacob-Israel is the bearer of divine election (Gen 32:28; 35:10). The juxtaposition with Esau reflects the Pauline theme of election (Rom 9:10-13). The subsequent lengthy treatment of Esau's line (vv. 35-54) before returning to Israel's line (ch. 2) follows the biblical pattern of dismissing the non-elect line first (cf. Ishmael in vv. 29-31 before Isaac).

verses.flow: The sons of Esau: Eliphaz, Reuel, Jeush, Jalam, and Korah.
The sons of Eliphaz: Teman, Omar, Zephi, Gatam, Kenaz, Timna, and Amalek.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Timna appears here as a son of Eliphaz (i.e., a clan/chief), whereas in verse 39 Timna is the sister of Lotan (a Horite/Seir clan). In Genesis 36:12, Timna is the concubine of Eliphaz who bore Amalek. The Chronicler lists Timna and Amalek as sons of Eliphaz, reflecting a practice of listing concubine-born sons and their mothers' names interchangeably with clan designations. THEOLOGICAL: Amalek's inclusion as a descendant of Esau explains the perennial enmity between Amalek and Israel (Exod 17:8-16; 1 Sam 15), rooted in the Esau-Jacob rivalry.

verses.flow: The sons of Reuel: Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, and Mizzah.
And the sons of Seir: Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah, Dishon, Ezer, and Dishan.
And the sons of Lotan: Hori and Homam; and the sister of Lotan was Timna.
The sons of Shobal: Alian, Manahath, Ebal, Shephi, and Onam. And the sons of Zibeon: Aiah and Anah.
The son of Anah: Dishon. And the sons of Dishon: Hamran, Eshban, Ithran, and Cheran.
The sons of Ezer: Bilhan, Zaavan, and Jaakan. The sons of Dishan: Uz and Aran.

verses.flow: Now these are the kings who reigned in the land of Edom before any king reigned over the sons of Israel: Bela the son of Beor, and the name of his city was Dinhabah.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "before any king reigned over the sons of Israel" is a prospective remark, indicating that the Chronicler is writing with full knowledge of the Israelite monarchy. This note grounds the Edomite king-list in redemptive-historical perspective: Edom had kings while Israel was still a tribal confederation under YHWH as their direct king. HISTORICAL: The Edomite king-list (vv. 43-50) finds a near-identical parallel in Genesis 36:31-39. These kings appear to be successive rulers rather than a dynasty, since each is described as reigning "in his place" with no father-son succession noted, suggesting election or conquest-based kingship.

verses.flow: And Husham died, and Hadad the son of Bedad, who struck Midian in the field of Moab, reigned in his place; and the name of his city was Avith.

verses.flow: And Baal-hanan died, and Hadad reigned in his place; and the name of his city was Pai, and his wife's name was Mehetabel the daughter of Matred, the daughter of Me-zahab.

verses.flow: And Hadad died. And the chiefs (allupim) of Edom were: chief Timna, chief Aliah, chief Jetheth,
verses.note: HEBREW: The term translated "chiefs" is allupim (singular: alluph, Strong's H441), derived from eleph meaning "thousand" or "clan," thus denoting a clan-chief or tribal leader. This is distinct from the word for king (melek), indicating a shift in Edom's political structure from monarchy to tribal chieftaincy by the time of the list in vv. 51-54. HISTORICAL: The transition from kings (vv. 43-50) to chiefs (allupim, vv. 51-54) likely reflects a historical shift in Edomite governance. A parallel list appears in Genesis 36:40-43. The twelve chiefs of Edom may intentionally parallel the twelve tribes of Israel, suggesting a structurally symmetrical but spiritually contrasting people.

verses.flow: chief Oholibamah, chief Elah, chief Pinon,
chief Kenaz, chief Teman, chief Mibzar,
chief Magdiel, chief Iram. These are the chiefs of Edom.
verses.note: STRUCTURE: The Edomite chief-list closes with the summary formula "These are the chiefs of Edom," mirroring the closing summaries of earlier sections. Ending the universal and Abrahamic genealogy with Edom rather than Israel is deliberate: the Chronicler will now pivot entirely to Israel's line beginning in chapter 2. Edom represents the end of the "rejected" lineages surveyed in chapter 1, all of which serve as foil to the elect line of Israel. TYPOLOGY: The dismissal of Edom's line points forward to the eschatological oracle against Edom (Obad 1; Isa 34; Mal 1:2-4), where YHWH declares His enduring election of Jacob over Esau, fulfilled ultimately in the universal kingdom of Israel's Messiah.

verses.flow: These are the sons of Israel: Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and Judah, Issachar, and Zebulun,
verses.note: STRUCTURE: The twelve sons of Israel (Jacob) are listed here as the foundation of the entire genealogical structure of 1 Chronicles. The ordering differs slightly from other biblical lists (Gen 35:22-26; Num 1), with Dan placed after Levi and Judah rather than with the Bilhah sons, possibly reflecting the Chronicler's theological arrangement. The list introduces the narrowing focus toward Judah and the Davidic line that dominates chapters 2-4.

verses.flow: Dan, Joseph, and Benjamin, Naphtali, Gad, and Asher.

conjunction + verb hiphil sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: The sons of Judah: Er, and Onan, and Shelah — three were born to him by the daughter of Shua the Canaanitess. And Er, the firstborn of Judah, was evil in the eyes of YHWH, and He put him to death.
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "evil in the eyes of YHWH" (ra' be'ene YHWH; H7451 + H5869 + H3068) is a standard covenantal formula indicating moral failure measured against YHWH's standard, not merely social convention. The Chronicler's brief mention of Er's death establishes divine accountability as operative even within the genealogy. TYPOLOGY: The immediate narrowing from Judah's firstborn to a younger line (through Perez) anticipates the consistent biblical pattern of the younger supplanting the elder by divine election (Rom 9:10-13).

verses.flow: And Tamar his daughter-in-law bore to him Perez and Zerah. All the sons of Judah were five.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Tamar's inclusion in the Judahite lineage — a woman who acted deceptively but was declared "more righteous" than Judah (Gen 38:26) — foreshadows the inclusion of unexpected figures in the Messiah's lineage. Matthew explicitly names Tamar in the genealogy of Jesus (Matt 1:3), underscoring that the Messiah's line is marked by grace overcoming scandal. Perez, born of this union, becomes the direct ancestor of David and ultimately of the Messiah.

verses.flow: The sons of Perez: Hezron and Hamul.
And the sons of Zerah: Zimri, and Ethan, and Heman, and Calcol, and Dara — five of them in all.
And the sons of Carmi: Achar, the troubler of Israel, who committed a trespass in the devoted thing.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Achar" (H5917) means "troubler," a deliberate wordplay on the name Achan (Josh 7). The root ('akar) is the same used by Joshua when he confronts Achan: "YHWH shall trouble you this day" (Josh 7:25). The Chronicler uses the name Achar rather than Achan to make the theological point permanent in the genealogy itself. THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "devoted thing" (cherem, H2764) refers to items placed under the ban of total consecration to YHWH; their misappropriation was treated as sacrilege against YHWH's direct claim on the spoils of holy war.

verses.flow: And the sons of Ethan: Azariah.
And the sons of Hezron who were born to him: Jerahmeel, and Ram, and Chelubai.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Chelubai" (H3621) is the same individual called "Caleb" later in the chapter (vv. 18, 42), a contracted form of the name. The threefold division of Hezron's sons — Jerahmeel, Ram, and Chelubai/Caleb — structures the entire genealogical section of chapter 2, with Ram's line leading to David (vv. 10-17), Jerahmeel's line filling vv. 25-41, and Caleb's line filling vv. 18-24, 42-55.

verses.flow: And Ram fathered Amminadab, and Amminadab fathered Nahshon, prince of the sons of Judah.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Nahshon the son of Amminadab was the prince (nasi, H5387) of Judah during the wilderness period (Num 1:7; 2:3) and played a prominent role in the dedication of the tabernacle (Num 7:12-17). His position as leader of Judah's tribe during the foundational wilderness generation underscores Judah's pre-eminence among the tribes. TYPOLOGY: This genealogical link is preserved in the NT messianic line (Matt 1:4; Luke 3:32-33).

verses.flow: And Nahshon fathered Salma, and Salma fathered Boaz.
And Boaz fathered Obed, and Obed fathered Jesse.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The line from Boaz to Obed to Jesse is the exact sequence found in Ruth 4:21-22, anchoring the book of Ruth within the grand Davidic genealogy. Boaz as kinsman-redeemer (go'el, H1350) typifies Christ as the one who redeems His people through covenantal obligation and love (hesed). The Chronicler's inclusion of this line affirms that the Gentile Moabitess Ruth is woven into the fabric of Israel's royal heritage and the Messiah's ancestry (Matt 1:5).

verses.flow: And Jesse fathered Eliab his firstborn, and Abinadab the second, and Shimea the third,
Nethanel the fourth, Raddai the fifth,
Ozem the sixth, David the seventh.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: David is listed as the seventh son here, whereas 1 Sam 16:10-11 and 17:12 suggest eight sons of Jesse. Scholars note that one son may have died without offspring and was thus omitted from the genealogical record, or the Chronicler is using a selective list. The number seven carried symbolic completeness in Hebrew thought, potentially underscoring David's role as the divinely chosen and complete king. The Chronicler's focus on David as the covenant king around whom the entire genealogical structure orbits is foundational to the theology of Chronicles.

verses.flow: And their sisters were Zeruiah and Abigail. And the sons of Zeruiah: Abishai, and Joab, and Asahel — three.
And Abigail bore Amasa, and the father of Amasa was Jether the Ishmaelite.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Jether the Ishmaelite (also called Ithra the Israelite in 2 Sam 17:25, likely a scribal variant) was the father of Amasa, whom Absalom appointed as army commander in place of Joab (2 Sam 17:25). The mention of Ishmaelite descent for Amasa adds a note of ethnic complexity to David's extended family network, reflecting the mixed heritage often present in the ancestral traditions of Israel's leading families.

verses.flow: And Caleb the son of Hezron fathered children by Azubah his wife, and by Jerioth; and these were her sons: Jesher, and Shobab, and Ardon.

verses.flow: And Azubah died, and Caleb took to himself Ephrath, who bore to him Hur.
And Hur fathered Uri, and Uri fathered Bezalel.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Bezalel the son of Uri the son of Hur was the Spirit-filled craftsman appointed by YHWH to construct the tabernacle (Exod 31:1-5; 35:30-35). His lineage from Caleb through Hur and Uri connects the Chronicler's genealogy to one of Israel's greatest acts of sacred craftsmanship. THEOLOGICAL: The filling of Bezalel with the ruach (H7307) of God for skilled craftsmanship is an early instance of the Spirit's enablement for specific vocational service, a theme developed throughout Scripture (cf. Eph 4:11-12).

verses.flow: And Geshur and Aram took from them the towns of Jair, with Kenath and its villages — sixty towns. All these were the sons of Machir the father of Gilead.

verses.flow: And after the death of Hezron in Caleb-ephrathah, Abijah the wife of Hezron bore to him Ashhur the father of Tekoa.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Caleb-ephrathah" is a compound place name of uncertain identification, possibly the area around Bethlehem-Ephrathah (Mic 5:2). The note about Hezron's death in Caleb-ephrathah may be providing geographical anchoring for the subsequent genealogical branches. Some scholars read the Hebrew here as "after Hezron died, Caleb went in to Ephrath the wife of Hezron his father," making Ashhur a posthumous son.

verses.flow: And the sons of Jerahmeel the firstborn of Hezron were: Ram the firstborn, and Bunah, and Oren, and Ozem, and Ahijah.

verses.flow: And Jerahmeel had another wife, whose name was Atarah; she was the mother of Onam.

verses.flow: And the sons of Ram the firstborn of Jerahmeel were: Maaz, and Jamin, and Eker.

verses.flow: And the sons of Onam were: Shammai and Jada. And the sons of Shammai: Nadab and Abishur.

verses.flow: And the name of the wife of Abishur was Abihail, and she bore to him Ahban and Molid.

verses.flow: And the sons of Nadab: Seled and Appaim; but Seled died without sons.
And the sons of Appaim: Ishi. And the sons of Ishi: Sheshan. And the sons of Sheshan: Ahlai.

verses.flow: And the sons of Jada the brother of Shammai: Jether and Jonathan; and Jether died without sons.
And the sons of Jonathan: Peleth and Zaza. These were the sons of Jerahmeel.
And Sheshan had no sons, only daughters. And Sheshan had an Egyptian servant whose name was Jarha.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The marriage of Sheshan's daughter to Jarha his Egyptian servant reflects legal and social practices within ancient Israel whereby a childless man might adopt a servant or give a daughter to a trusted slave to perpetuate the family line. This practice has parallels in ancient Near Eastern law codes (e.g., the Nuzi tablets) where slave adoption for inheritance purposes is well-attested. The resulting lineage (vv. 35-41) extends thirteen generations, suggesting this section preserves an unusually detailed clan record.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd fem sing

verses.flow: And Sheshan gave his daughter to Jarha his servant as wife, and she bore to him Attai.
And Attai fathered Nathan, and Nathan fathered Zabad.
And Zabad fathered Ephlal, and Ephlal fathered Obed.
And Obed fathered Jehu, and Jehu fathered Azariah.
And Azariah fathered Helez, and Helez fathered Eleasah.
And Eleasah fathered Sismai, and Sismai fathered Shallum.
And Shallum fathered Jekamiah, and Jekamiah fathered Elishama.
And the sons of Caleb the brother of Jerahmeel: Mesha his firstborn, who was the father of Ziph; and the sons of Mareshah the father of Hebron.
verses.note: STRUCTURE: The genealogy of Caleb the brother of Jerahmeel (vv. 42-55) is distinct from the Caleb son of Jephunneh of the tribe of Judah who received Hebron as his inheritance (Josh 14:13-14), though some traditions may conflate them. The Chronicler presents multiple Calebite sub-clans whose settlement patterns across southern Judah (Hebron, Ziph, Tekoa, Bethlehem, Kiriath-jearim) reflect the tribal geography of Judah that was vital for understanding the land theology central to Chronicles.

verses.flow: And the sons of Hebron: Korah, and Tappuah, and Rekem, and Shema.
And Shema fathered Raham the father of Jorkeam, and Rekem fathered Shammai.
And the son of Shammai was Maon, and Maon was the father of Beth-zur.
And Ephah, the concubine of Caleb, bore Haran, and Moza, and Gazez; and Haran fathered Gazez.
And the sons of Jahdai: Regem, and Jotham, and Geshan, and Pelet, and Ephah, and Shaaph.
Maacah, the concubine of Caleb, bore Sheber and Tirhanah.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd fem sing

verses.flow: And she bore also Shaaph the father of Madmannah, Sheva the father of Machbenah and the father of Gibea; and the daughter of Caleb was Achsah.
These were the sons of Caleb, the son of Hur the firstborn of Ephrathah: Shobal the father of Kiriath-jearim,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Kiriath-jearim (meaning "city of forests") was the place where the ark of the covenant rested for twenty years after its return from the Philistines (1 Sam 7:1-2; 2 Sam 6:2), making it a theologically significant location in the lead-up to David's transfer of the ark to Jerusalem. The Chronicler's tracing of Kiriath-jearim's founding clan connects the Calebite genealogy to the ark's history — a central theme in 1 Chronicles 13-16.

verses.flow: Salma the father of Bethlehem, Hareph the father of Beth-gader.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Salma (or Salmon) as "father of Bethlehem" anchors this Calebite clan to the town that would become the birthplace of David (1 Sam 17:12) and, supremely, of the Messiah Jesus (Mic 5:2; Matt 2:1; Luke 2:4-7). The Chronicler's genealogical notation that a descendant of Judah through Hezron founded or settled Bethlehem provides the clan continuity underlying the messianic significance of that city.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc plur

verses.flow: And Shobal the father of Kiriath-jearim had sons: Haroeh, half of the Menuhoth.
And the families of Kiriath-jearim: the Ithrites, and the Puthites, and the Shumathites, and the Mishraites; from these came the Zorathites and the Eshtaolites.
The sons of Salma: Bethlehem, and the Netophathites, Atroth-beth-joab, and half of the Manahathites, the Zorites.
And the families of the scribes who dwelt at Jabez: the Tirathites, the Shimeathites, the Sucathites. These are the Kenites who came from Hammath, the father of the house of Rechab.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "families of scribes" (mishpechot sopherim, H4940 + H5608) dwelling at Jabez represents a remarkable detail — a scribal guild attached to a Kenite clan. The Kenites were descendants of Moses' father-in-law Jethro (Judg 1:16), a non-Israelite people who attached themselves to Israel and were known for covenant fidelity (1 Sam 15:6). DIVINE_COUNCIL: The house of Rechab (Rechabites) later becomes notable for their radical covenant loyalty (Jer 35), and YHWH promises them a perpetual "standing before Me" — covenantal language of priestly and council access. The inclusion of Kenites within the genealogy of Judah testifies to the ingathering of the nations into Israel's covenantal orbit, a harbinger of the eschatological vision (Isa 56:6-7; Rev 5:9).

verses.flow: Now these were the sons of David who were born to him in Hebron: the firstborn, Amnon, by Ahinoam the Jezreelitess; the second, Daniel, by Abigail the Carmelitess;
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The list of David's sons born in Hebron reflects his seven-and-a-half year reign there (2 Sam 2:11) before capturing Jerusalem. Hebron was the ancient city associated with the patriarchal covenant (Gen 13:18), making it a fitting seat of early Davidic kingship. The name "Daniel" in Chronicles replaces "Chileab" in 2 Sam 3:3, possibly a throne name or variant tradition.

the fifth, Shephatiah, by Abital; the sixth, Ithream, by his wife Eglah.

verses.flow: Six were born to him in Hebron, and he reigned there seven years and six months. And thirty-three years he reigned in Jerusalem.
And these were born to him in Jerusalem: Shimea, and Shobab, and Nathan, and Solomon — four, by Bath-shua the daughter of Ammiel;
verses.note: HEBREW: "Bath-shua" (bat-shua) means "daughter of prosperity" or "daughter of a cry for help" — this is the Chronicler's form of "Bathsheba" (bat-sheva). The listing of Nathan alongside Solomon is theologically significant, as the Messiah Yeshua's lineage in Luke 3:31 traces through Nathan rather than Solomon, while Matthew 1:6 traces the legal royal line through Solomon. TYPOLOGY: The inclusion of Nathan in the Davidic genealogy points forward to the dual lineage of Messiah — both royal heir and priestly/prophetic line converging.

verses.flow: and Ibhar, and Elishama, and Eliphelet,
and Nogah, and Nepheg, and Japhia,
and Elishama, and Eliada, and Eliphelet — nine.
All these were the sons of David, besides the sons of the concubines; and Tamar was their sister.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The explicit mention of Tamar among the sons signals the Chronicler's awareness of the moral catastrophes within David's household (2 Sam 13), yet the line is preserved by divine grace (hesed). The reference to "sons of the concubines" acknowledges the complex dynastic realities while keeping focus on the covenantal line through the recognized wives.

verses.flow: And the son of Solomon was Rehoboam; Abijah his son, Asa his son, Jehoshaphat his son,
verses.note: STRUCTURE: Verses 10-16 constitute the royal Davidic succession from Solomon to the Babylonian exile — twenty generations of kings. The Chronicler presents this list in a tightly compressed genealogical form, each "his son" (beno) linking the chain of covenant promise. This mirrors the function of Matthew 1's genealogy, which draws directly from this passage to establish Messiah's royal credentials.

verses.flow: Joram his son, Ahaziah his son, Joash his son,
Amaziah his son, Azariah his son, Jotham his son,
Ahaz his son, Hezekiah his son, Manasseh his son,
Amon his son, Josiah his son.
And the sons of Josiah: the firstborn, Johanan; the second, Jehoiakim; the third, Zedekiah; the fourth, Shallum.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The birth order of Josiah's sons is significant: Shallum is identified as the fourth son yet became king as Jehoahaz (2 Kgs 23:30-31), suggesting he was chosen by popular acclamation over his older brothers. Jehoiakim (Eliakim) was installed by Pharaoh Necho (2 Kgs 23:34), highlighting the political turbulence surrounding the fall of Jerusalem. Zedekiah here is a different person from the Zedekiah of v.16.

verses.flow: And the sons of Jehoiakim: Jeconiah his son, Zedekiah his son.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jeconiah (also called Coniah and Jehoiachin) is the king over whom YHWH pronounced a curse: "no man of his offspring shall succeed in sitting on the throne of David" (Jer 22:30). This creates a profound theological tension resolved only in Messiah — Joseph's legal paternity confers royal title through Jeconiah's line (Matt 1:12) while the biological lineage through Mary via Nathan (Luke 3:27) bypasses the curse. TYPOLOGY: The captivity of Jeconiah represents the nadir of the Davidic dynasty, from which only miraculous divine intervention could restore the throne.

verses.flow: And the sons of Jeconiah the captive: Shealtiel his son,
verses.note: HEBREW: "Jeconiah the captive" (asir) — the Hebrew word 'asir' (Strong's H615) means "prisoner" or "bound one." This is a title of deep pathos: the heir of David's eternal throne sits in Babylonian chains. Yet from this captive comes Shealtiel, and from Shealtiel's line comes Zerubbabel, the governor of the restoration — a picture of resurrection from covenantal death.

verses.flow: and Malchiram, and Pedaiah, and Shenazzar, Jekamiah, Hoshama, and Nedabiah.
And the sons of Pedaiah: Zerubbabel and Shimei. And the sons of Zerubbabel: Meshullam and Hananiah, and Shelomith was their sister;
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Zerubbabel stands as one of the most significant messianic types in the entire Old Testament. As the Davidic heir who led the return from exile and oversaw the rebuilding of the temple, he is addressed directly in Haggai 2:23 as YHWH's "signet ring" — the very language reversed from the curse on Jeconiah (Jer 22:24). DIVINE_COUNCIL: The Spirit (ruach) of YHWH empowers Zerubbabel for the temple task (Zech 4:6), pointing to the ultimate Temple-builder, Messiah, who would rebuild the dwelling place of God in human flesh (John 2:19-21).

verses.flow: and Hashubah, and Ohel, and Berechiah, and Hasadiah, Jushab-hesed — five.
verses.note: HEBREW: The name "Jushab-hesed" (yushab-hesed) is compound: "hesed" (Strong's H2617) is the great covenantal word meaning steadfast love, loyal-kindness, covenant-faithfulness — and "yushab" means "returned." Thus the name means "hesed (steadfast love) has returned," an extraordinarily fitting name for a child of the restoration generation, born as living testimony that YHWH's covenant love had not abandoned His people in exile.

verses.flow: And the sons of Hananiah: Pelatiah and Jeshaiah, the sons of Rephaiah, the sons of Arnan, the sons of Obadiah, the sons of Shecaniah.
verses.note: STRUCTURE: The genealogy in vv.21-24 extends into the post-exilic period and likely reaches into the fifth or fourth century BC, making this one of the latest-dated genealogical records in the Hebrew Bible. The phrase "sons of Rephaiah, sons of Arnan, sons of Obadiah, sons of Shecaniah" follows a pattern of listing descendants through successive clan heads, tracing the Davidic line's survival after the collapse of the monarchy.

verses.flow: And the sons of Shecaniah: Shemaiah. And the sons of Shemaiah: Hattush, and Igal, and Bariah, and Neariah, and Shaphat — six.
And the sons of Neariah: Elioenai, and Hizkiah, and Azrikam — three.
And the sons of Elioenai: Hodaviah, and Eliashib, and Pelaiah, and Akkub, and Johanan, and Delaiah, and Anani — seven.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The list ends with Anani, the seventh son of Elioenai — seven being the number of completeness in Hebrew thought. Remarkably, some Jewish interpreters (Talmud Sanhedrin 38a) identified "Anani" with the "one coming with the clouds" in Daniel 7:13, connecting the name's possible derivation from 'anan (cloud). Whether or not this identification is correct, the genealogy's preservation into the post-exilic era testifies to Israel's unbroken hope: the Davidic line survived exile and dispersion, awaiting the day when YHWH would raise up the righteous Branch (Jer 23:5) to reign forever.

verses.flow: The sons of Judah: Perez, Hezron, Carmi, Hur, and Shobal.
verses.note: STRUCTURE: The genealogy of Judah in chapter 4 resumes the line begun in 2:3-4:23, expanding on collateral branches. The five names listed — Perez, Hezron, Carmi, Hur, and Shobal — do not follow a strictly linear descent but survey the major tribal sub-clans. The Chronicler's selective genealogy emphasizes Judah's primacy among the tribes, pointing toward the Davidic covenant and ultimately the Messiah.

verses.flow: And Reaiah the son of Shobal fathered Jahath, and Jahath fathered Ahumai and Lahad. These are the clans of the Zorathites.
And these were the sons of the father of Etam: Jezreel, Ishma, and Idbash; and the name of their sister was Hazzelelponi.
And Penuel was the father of Gedor, and Ezer was the father of Hushah. These are the sons of Hur, the firstborn of Ephrathah, the father of Bethlehem.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Bethlehem is explicitly named as the inheritance of Hur's firstborn line. The Chronicler's deliberate mention of Bethlehem (Hebrew: Beit Lechem, "house of bread") anticipates the birth of David (1 Sam 17:12) and of YHWH's anointed king (Mic 5:2; Matt 2:1-6). The genealogical rooting of Bethlehem in the tribe of Judah underscores the covenantal continuity of the Davidic promise.

verses.flow: And Ashhur, the father of Tekoa, had two wives: Helah and Naarah.

verses.flow: And Jabez was more honored than his brothers; and his mother called his name Jabez, saying, "Because I bore him in pain (etzev)."
verses.note: HEBREW: The name "Jabez" (Hebrew: Ya'betz, Strong's H3258) is a wordplay on the word "etzev" (H6093), meaning "pain," "sorrow," or "toil." The mother's naming of her son in sorrow echoes the curse language of Gen 3:16-17, where the same root describes the pain of childbearing and laborious toil. The name thus carries a burden of suffering that Jabez's prayer seeks to transcend.

verses.flow: And Jabez called upon the God of Israel, saying, "Oh that you would bless me indeed and enlarge my border, and that your hand would be with me, and that you would keep me from harm so that it might not bring me pain (etzev)!" And God granted what he requested.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The prayer of Jabez is one of the most compressed and theologically rich petitions in Chronicles. He appeals directly to "the God of Israel" — a covenantal title — requesting blessing, expanded territory, divine accompaniment, and protection from harm. The fourfold request mirrors the covenantal blessings of Deut 28. Critically, God "granted what he requested" (Hebrew: wayyave' elohim et asher sha'al), affirming that earnest covenantal prayer moves the hand of YHWH. The verse should not be reduced to a prosperity formula but stands as an example of bold, faith-driven petition within covenant relationship.

verses.flow: And Chelub the brother of Shuhah fathered Mehir, who was the father of Eshton.
And Eshton fathered Beth-rapha, Paseah, and Tehinnah the father of Ir-nahash. These are the men of Recah.
And the sons of Kenaz: Othniel and Seraiah. And the sons of Othniel: Hathath and Meonothai.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Othniel son of Kenaz is the first judge of Israel (Judg 3:9-11), a Gentile-descended clan (Kenizzites) absorbed into Judah. His inclusion in the Judahite genealogy demonstrates the Chronicler's inclusive vision of covenant identity, where faithfulness — not mere ethnic origin — defines membership in YHWH's people. Othniel's role as deliverer also carries typological weight as a forerunner of YHWH's appointed saviors.

And the sons of Caleb the son of Jephunneh: Iru, Elah, and Naam; and the sons of Elah: Kenaz.
And the sons of Jehallelel: Ziph, Ziphah, Tiria, and Asarel.

verses.flow: And the sons of Ezrah: Jether, Mered, Epher, and Jalon. And she conceived and bore Miriam, Shammai, and Ishbah the father of Eshtemoa.
And his Judahite wife bore Jered the father of Gedor, Heber the father of Soco, and Jekuthiel the father of Zanoah. And these are the sons of Bithiah the daughter of Pharaoh, whom Mered took.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The mention of "Bithiah the daughter of Pharaoh" is striking — an Egyptian princess incorporated into the tribe of Judah through marriage to Mered. Rabbinic tradition (b. Megillah 13a) identifies her with the daughter of Pharaoh who rescued Moses (Exod 2:5-10), though this cannot be confirmed textually. The inclusion of an Egyptian royal into the genealogy of Judah reflects the Chronicler's theology of the nations being drawn into YHWH's covenantal community, anticipating the eschatological ingathering.

verses.flow: And the sons of the wife of Hodiah, the sister of Naham: the father of Keilah the Garmite and Eshtemoa the Maacathite.
And the sons of Shimon: Amnon, Rinnah, Ben-hanan, and Tilon. And the sons of Ishi: Zoheth and Ben-zoheth.
The sons of Shelah the son of Judah: Er the father of Lecah, and Laadah the father of Mareshah, and the clans of the house of linen workers at Beth-ashbea,
verses.note: HEBREW: "Shelah the son of Judah" recalls the complicated narrative of Gen 38, where Shelah was withheld from Tamar by Judah. The Chronicler rehabilitates Shelah's line without comment on the past, focusing on his descendants' vocational identity — linen workers and craftsmen. The phrase "house of linen workers" (Hebrew: beit avodat ha-butz) suggests an established guild, possibly connected to priestly linen production (Exod 39:27-29), lending this obscure line possible tabernacle/temple significance.

verses.flow: and Jokim, and the men of Cozeba, and Joash, and Saraph, who ruled in Moab and returned to Lehem (and these are ancient records).
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "and these are ancient records" (Hebrew: veha-devarim attikim, lit. "and the words/matters are ancient") is a rare scribal notation acknowledging the antiquity and uncertainty of the source material. This kind of meta-textual comment is uncommon in biblical genealogies and reflects the Chronicler's careful, scholarly approach to his sources, preserving even partially obscure traditions.

verses.flow: These were the potters and inhabitants of Netaim and Gederah; they dwelt there with the king in his service.
The sons of Simeon: Nemuel, Jamin, Jarib, Zerah, and Shaul;
Shallum his son, Mibsam his son, Mishma his son.
And the sons of Mishma: Hamuel his son, Zaccur his son, Shimei his son.
And Shimei had sixteen sons and six daughters; but his brothers did not have many sons, nor did all their clan multiply like the sons of Judah.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The explicit notation that Simeon did not multiply "like the sons of Judah" reflects the covenantal diminishment of Simeon prophesied in Gen 49:7, where Simeon and Levi are scattered in Israel as a consequence of their violence at Shechem (Gen 34). The Chronicler records this reduction matter-of-factly, demonstrating that the consequences of ancestral sin can shape generational demographic realities within the covenant people.

verses.flow: And they found rich and good pasture, and the land was very broad and quiet and peaceful (shalom); for the former inhabitants there had belonged to Ham.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The note that the prior inhabitants "belonged to Ham" (Hebrew: min-Ham) invokes the Table of Nations theology of Gen 10 and the cursed line of Canaan (Gen 9:25). In the worldview of Second Temple Judaism informed by Deuteronomy 32:8-9, the nations were allotted to the sons of God (divine beings) while Israel remained YHWH's portion. The Hamitic peoples' presence in the land represents territory under rival divine administration, which Israel's expansion here displaces under YHWH's authority.

verses.flow: And these, written by name, came in the days of Hezekiah king of Judah, and struck down their tents and the Meunim who were found there, and devoted them to destruction (herem) to this day, and settled in their place, because there was pasture there for their flocks.
verses.note: HEBREW: The term "devoted them to destruction" translates the Hebrew "herem" (H2764), the sacred ban of total destruction consecrated to YHWH. This term carries deep covenantal and warfare theology: herem removes the devoted object or people from ordinary use and consecrates it to YHWH, often through destruction. Its application here in the days of Hezekiah — centuries after the conquest — suggests that Simeon continued to understand its territorial expansion in terms of holy war under YHWH's mandate.

verses.flow: And they struck the remnant of the Amalekites who had escaped, and they have dwelt there to this day.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The annihilation of the remnant of the Amalekites fulfills the mandate of 1 Sam 15 and Exod 17:14-16, where YHWH declared perpetual war against Amalek. Saul's failure to execute herem against Agag (1 Sam 15) is implicitly contrasted with Simeon's completion of this ancient task. The phrase "to this day" indicates the Chronicler is drawing on a living tradition, anchoring the narrative in historical memory. Amalek in prophetic typology represents persistent opposition to YHWH's kingdom purposes (Num 24:20).

verses.flow: And the sons of Reuben the firstborn of Israel — for he was the firstborn, but because he defiled the bed of his father, his birthright was given to the sons of Joseph the son of Israel, so that he is not enrolled in the genealogy according to the birthright.
verses.note: HEBREW: The term "birthright" (bekorah, H1062) refers to the legal right of the firstborn to a double inheritance and family leadership. Reuben's forfeiture (Gen 35:22; 49:3-4) due to his defilement of Bilhah's bed is a foundational explanation for the unexpected transfer of tribal preeminence. The Chronicler notes this to frame the ensuing genealogies theologically rather than strictly by birth order. THEOLOGICAL: The birthright's transfer to Joseph (specifically through Ephraim and Manasseh) while leadership passes to Judah illustrates the sovereign divine reordering of human expectations, pointing toward the Messiah's descent through Judah (Gen 49:10).

verses.flow: For Judah prevailed over his brothers, and from him came the one who is ruler (nagid); but the birthright belonged to Joseph —
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The term "nagid" (H5057, ruler/prince/designated leader) is a specifically royal and messianic title used of David (1 Sam 13:14) and ultimately points to the coming Davidic King, Jesus the Messiah, who descends from Judah (Matt 1:2-3; Rev 5:5). THEOLOGICAL: The Chronicler's careful distinction — Judah holds the scepter of leadership, but Joseph holds the double-portion of inheritance — reflects the complexity of covenant fulfillment and the intentionality of divine election across tribal lines.

verses.flow: the sons of Reuben the firstborn of Israel: Hanoch, and Pallu, Hezron, and Carmi.
The sons of Joel: Shemaiah his son, Gog his son, Shimei his son,
Micah his son, Reaiah his son, Baal his son,
Beerah his son, whom Tilgath-pilneser king of Assyria carried away into exile; he was a chief (nasi) of the Reubenites.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Tilgath-pilneser III (745–727 BC), also known as Pul (his throne name in Babylon, cf. v. 26; 2 Kgs 15:19), was the Assyrian king who initiated the policy of mass deportation of conquered peoples to dissolve ethnic and national identities. His campaigns against Transjordanian tribes are corroborated by Assyrian annals. The Reubenites were among the first of Israel's tribes to suffer exile, a consequence of their eastern, exposed position. THEOLOGICAL: The exile of Beerah is set as a direct consequence of unfaithfulness (cf. v. 25), linking genealogical record with theological cause and effect.

verses.flow: And in the days of Saul they made war against the Hagrites, who fell by their hand, and they dwelt in their tents throughout all the region east of Gilead.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The Hagrites were a semi-nomadic people of northern Arabia, likely connected to Hagar (Gen 16), dwelling east of Gilead. This war during Saul's reign is otherwise unattested in Samuel-Kings, making Chronicles a unique historical source. The capture of their "tents" indicates the displacement of a pastoral nomadic people from their grazing territories.

verses.flow: And they lived in Gilead, in Bashan and in its towns, and in all the pasturelands of Sharon to their limits.

This is notable as Jotham and Jeroboam II were kings of rival kingdoms, suggesting the Chronicler drew from official administrative census records from both Judah and Israel.

TYPOLOGY: This battle mirrors the principle articulated in (2 Chr 20:15): "the battle is not yours but God's," a theme fulfilled eschatologically in Messiah's victory over all enemies (Rev 19:11-21).

verses.flow: But they broke faith with the God of their fathers, and whored after the gods of the peoples of the land, whom God had destroyed before them.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The verb "whored" (zanah, H2181) describes covenant infidelity in explicitly marital-covenantal terms, drawing on the prophetic metaphor of Israel as YHWH's bride who commits spiritual adultery by following other gods (Hos 1-3; Ezek 16). verses.flow: So the God of Israel stirred up the spirit (ruach) of Pul king of Assyria, the spirit (ruach) of Tilgath-pilneser king of Assyria, and he carried them away into exile, namely the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh, and brought them to Halah, Habor, Hara, and the river Gozan, to this day.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Stirred up the spirit (ruach, H7307)" — the verb "ur" (H5782, to rouse/stir up) is used of YHWH's sovereign activation of human agents to accomplish his purposes. YHWH moves even the ruach of a pagan Assyrian king, demonstrating that all rulers — even those who do not acknowledge him — serve as instruments of divine judgment or restoration. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The divine stirring of Pul's spirit recalls the broader OT pattern where YHWH exercises supreme authority over all earthly kings by direct action upon their innermost will (Prov 21:1; Ezra 1:1). The exile to Halah, Habor, Hara, and the river Gozan is historically confirmed in Assyrian deportation records. TYPOLOGY: The exile foreshadows the final scattering of covenant breakers, while the later return under Cyrus (2 Chr 36:22-23) typologically anticipates the eschatological ingathering of Israel under the Messiah (Isa 11:11-12).

verses.flow: The sons of Levi: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari.
verses.note: HEBREW: The Hebrew name "Levi" (levi, Strong's H3878) means "joined" or "attached," rooted in the account of Leah's naming in Gen 29:34. The three sons listed here — Gershon, Kohath, and Merari — form the organizational backbone of the entire Levitical system, and their clans determine the division of sacred duties throughout the Pentateuch (Num 3-4).

verses.flow: The sons of Kohath: Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel.
The children of Amram: Aaron, Moses, and Miriam. The sons of Aaron: Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The inclusion of Miriam alongside Aaron and Moses is notable — she is recognized as a co-leader in the Exodus narrative (Mic 6:4) and as a prophetess (Exod 15:20). The four sons of Aaron are all named, including Nadab and Abihu, whose deaths for offering unauthorized fire before YHWH (Lev 10:1-2) resulted in the priestly line continuing through Eleazar and Ithamar.

verses.flow: Eleazar fathered Phinehas, Phinehas fathered Abishua,
Abishua fathered Bukki, Bukki fathered Uzzi,
Uzzi fathered Zerahiah, Zerahiah fathered Meraioth,
Meraioth fathered Amariah, Amariah fathered Ahitub,
Ahitub fathered Zadok, Zadok fathered Ahimaaz,
Ahimaaz fathered Azariah, Azariah fathered Johanan,
Johanan fathered Azariah — he is the one who served as priest in the house that Solomon built in Jerusalem —
verses.note: HISTORICAL: This parenthetical note identifying Azariah as the priest who served in Solomon's Temple provides a crucial chronological anchor, linking the genealogical list to the historical moment of the Temple's construction (c. 960 BCE). The Chronicler inserts such notes to demonstrate the unbroken continuity of legitimate priestly service from Aaron through the monarchy period.

verses.flow: Azariah fathered Amariah, Amariah fathered Ahitub,
Ahitub fathered Zadok, Zadok fathered Shallum,
Shallum fathered Hilkiah, Hilkiah fathered Azariah,
Azariah fathered Seraiah, Seraiah fathered Jehozadak;
and Jehozadak went into exile when YHWH carried away Judah and Jerusalem by the hand of Nebuchadnezzar.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "YHWH carried away (galah, Strong's H1540) Judah and Jerusalem" frames the Babylonian exile as a direct act of YHWH's sovereign judgment rather than merely a political or military event. The preservation of Jehozadak's name in the priestly genealogy is theologically significant: his son Jeshua (Joshua) would return with Zerubbabel and resume legitimate priestly ministry (Ezra 2:2; Hag 1:1), demonstrating YHWH's faithfulness across the devastation of exile.

verses.flow: These are the names of the sons of Gershom: Libni and Shimei.
The sons of Kohath: Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel.
The sons of Merari: Mahli and Mushi. These are the clans of the Levites according to their fathers.
Of Gershom: Libni his son, Jahath his son, Zimmah his son,
Joah his son, Iddo his son, Zerah his son, Jeatherai his son.
The sons of Kohath: Amminadab his son, Korah his son, Assir his son,
Elkanah his son, Ebiasaph his son, Assir his son,
Tahath his son, Uriel his son, Uzziah his son, and Shaul his son.
The sons of Elkanah: Amasai and Ahimoth,
Elkanah his son, Zophai his son, Nahath his son,
Eliab his son, Jeroham his son, Elkanah his son.
The sons of Samuel: Joel the firstborn, and Abijah the second.
The sons of Merari: Mahli, Libni his son, Shimei his son, Uzzah his son,
Shimea his son, Haggiah his son, Asaiah his son.
These are the ones whom David appointed over the service of song in the house of YHWH, after the ark rested there.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: David's appointment of Levitical singers represents a significant theological development — the formalization of sacred music as a covenantal institution within Israelite worship. The phrase "after the ark rested" (nuach, Strong's H5117) evokes the language of Sabbath rest and anticipates the permanent rest the ark would find in Solomon's Temple. This Davidic ordering of worship is presented throughout Chronicles as authoritative and binding.

verses.flow: They ministered with song before the tabernacle of the tent of meeting, until Solomon built the house of YHWH in Jerusalem, and they served according to their order in their service.

verses.flow: These are the ones who served, and their sons: Of the sons of the Kohathites: Heman the singer, the son of Joel, the son of Samuel,
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Heman the singer is identified as a descendant of Samuel, who was himself a descendant of Kohath. The lineage traced here — through Kohath, Izhar, and ultimately to Levi and Israel — grounds the worship leadership in the very covenant history of the nation. Heman is likely the author of Psalm 88, a psalm of profound lament, indicating that even the formally appointed temple singers wrestled deeply with grief and divine hiddenness.

verses.flow: the son of Elkanah, the son of Jeroham, the son of Eliel, the son of Toah,
the son of Zuph, the son of Elkanah, the son of Mahath, the son of Amasai,
the son of Elkanah, the son of Joel, the son of Azariah, the son of Zephaniah,
the son of Tahath, the son of Assir, the son of Ebiasaph, the son of Korah,
the son of Izhar, the son of Kohath, the son of Levi, the son of Israel.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The chain "Levi, the son of Israel" anchors the entire Levitical genealogy in the patriarchal covenant. In the theological framework of Deut 32:8-9, Israel is YHWH's own inheritance among the nations, and Levi — set apart with no territorial inheritance — is doubly consecrated as the portion belonging to YHWH within his own people. This mirrors the structure of divine council theology where YHWH retains special possession even within his allotted domain.

verses.flow: And his brother Asaph, who stood at his right hand: Asaph the son of Berechiah, the son of Shimea,
verses.note: HEBREW: The verb "stood" (amad, Strong's H5975) in a liturgical context carries the technical sense of officiating or taking one's appointed station in the divine presence (cf. Deut 10:8; 2 Chr 29:11). The spatial arrangement — Heman at center, Asaph on his right, Ethan on his left — reflects a deliberate hierarchical and cultic order in the worship of YHWH.

verses.flow: But Aaron and his sons made offerings upon the altar of burnt offering and upon the altar of incense, for all the work of the most holy place, and to make atonement for Israel, according to all that Moses the servant of God had commanded.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse functions as a theological hinge within the chapter, distinguishing the broader Levitical service from the exclusive Aaronic prerogatives: burnt offerings (olah, Strong's H5930), incense (qetoret, Strong's H7004), and atonement (kaphar, Strong's H3722). The phrase "most holy place" (qodesh haqqodashim) marks the innermost sanctum as the locus of Israel's reconciliation with YHWH. The reference to "Moses the servant of God" invokes Mosaic authority as the permanent warrant for Aaronic priestly order (cf. Heb 5:4).

verses.flow: Meraioth his son, Amariah his son, Ahitub his son,
Zadok his son, Ahimaaz his son.
Now these are their dwelling places according to their encampments within their borders: to the sons of Aaron, of the clans of the Kohathites — for the lot fell to them —
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The allocation of cities to Levitical clans by lot (goral, Strong's H1486) reflects the theological conviction that the land distribution was divinely determined rather than politically negotiated. The Levites received no contiguous tribal territory (cf. Num 18:20) but were instead dispersed throughout the nation in forty-eight cities (Num 35:7), ensuring that priestly instruction and worship leadership would permeate the entire covenant community.

verses.flow: to them they gave Hebron in the land of Judah and its surrounding pasturelands.
But the fields of the city and its villages they gave to Caleb the son of Jephunneh.
To the sons of Aaron they gave the cities of refuge: Hebron, Libnah with its pasturelands, Jattir, Eshtemoa with its pasturelands,
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Hebron's designation as a city of refuge (ir miqlatah) is significant: it was previously the ancient city of Arba, associated with the Anakim (Josh 14:15), and was given first to Caleb as a military reward. The Levites received the city itself while Caleb retained the surrounding agricultural lands (v. 56), illustrating the careful balance between warrior reward and priestly provision in the settlement of Canaan.

verses.flow: Hilen with its pasturelands, Debir with its pasturelands,
Ashan with its pasturelands, and Beth-shemesh with its pasturelands;
and from the tribe of Benjamin: Geba with its pasturelands, Allemeth with its pasturelands, and Anathoth with its pasturelands. All their cities throughout their clans were thirteen cities.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Anathoth, listed here among the Levitical cities in Benjamin, would later become the birthplace of the prophet Jeremiah (Jer 1:1), whose priestly lineage thus traces back to the Aaronic line settled in this city. This connection grounds prophetic ministry in the same Levitical institution that produced Israel's priesthood, blurring the later distinction between priest and prophet in the person of Jeremiah.

verses.flow: To the rest of the sons of Kohath were given by lot, out of the clan of the tribe, out of the half-tribe, the half of Manasseh, ten cities.

verses.flow: To the sons of Gershom, according to their clans, were allotted thirteen cities out of the tribes of Issachar, Asher, Naphtali, and Manasseh in Bashan.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The Gershomite cities include territory in Bashan — a region carrying heavy spiritual resonance in ancient Israelite cosmology. Bashan was associated with Og and the Rephaim, and in Second Temple tradition was viewed as a gateway to the realm of the dead (cf. Ps 68:22; Deut 3:11). The Levitical settlement of Gershomite families in Bashan represents the extension of covenantal order into a territory previously associated with anti-divine powers.

verses.flow: To the sons of Merari, according to their clans, were allotted twelve cities out of the tribes of Reuben, Gad, and Zebulun.
So the people of Israel gave the Levites the cities with their pasturelands.
They gave by lot out of the tribes of Judah, Simeon, and Benjamin these cities which are mentioned by name.
And some of the clans of the sons of Kohath had cities of their territory out of the tribe of Ephraim.
Now the sons of Issachar: Tola, and Puah, Jashub, and Shimron — four.
verses.note: HEBREW: The name "Issachar" (Yissachar, Strong's H3485) is traditionally understood to mean "there is reward" or "he will bring reward," connecting to the account of his birth in Gen 30:18. The four sons listed here correspond to the four clan-heads of Issachar (cf. Num 26:23-25), establishing the genealogy's continuity with the Pentateuchal record.

verses.flow: And the sons of Tola: Uzzi, and Rephaiah, and Jeriel, and Jahmai, and Ibsam, and Shemuel, heads of their fathers' houses, belonging to Tola, mighty men of valor in their generations — their number in the days of David was twenty-two thousand six hundred.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The census figure of 22,600 for Tola's descendants "in the days of David" anchors this genealogy to the Davidic era, suggesting these registers were drawn from royal administrative records. The phrase "mighty men of valor" (gibborim, Strong's H1368) echoes the language used for David's elite warriors, connecting tribal identity to military readiness.

verses.flow: And the son of Uzzi: Izrahiah; and the sons of Izrahiah: Michael, and Obadiah, and Joel, and Isshiah — five, all of them chief men.
And with them, by their generations, according to their fathers' houses, were thirty-six thousand troops ready for war; for they had many wives and sons.
And their kinsmen belonging to all the families of Issachar, mighty men of valor — eighty-seven thousand, all of them enrolled by genealogy.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The total of 87,000 "enrolled by genealogy" (hithyahes, Strong's H3187) for Issachar underscores the Chronicler's persistent emphasis on legitimate covenantal lineage as the basis for participation in the assembly of Israel. The Hebrew root yhs carries the sense of formal registration establishing identity and inheritance rights within the covenant community.

verses.flow: The sons of Benjamin: Bela, and Becher, and Jediael — three.

verses.flow: And the sons of Bela: Ezbon, and Uzzi, and Uzziel, and Jerimoth, and Iri — five, heads of fathers' houses, mighty men of valor; and their enrollment by genealogy was twenty-two thousand thirty-four.
And the sons of Becher: Zemirah, and Joash, and Eliezer, and Elioenai, and Omri, and Jeremoth, and Abijah, and Anathoth, and Alemeth — all these were the sons of Becher.

verses.flow: And their enrollment by genealogy, according to their generations, as heads of their fathers' houses, mighty men of valor — twenty thousand two hundred.
And the son of Jediael: Bilhan; and the sons of Bilhan: Jeush, and Benjamin, and Ehud, and Chenaanah, and Zethan, and Tarshish, and Ahishahar.
All these were sons of Jediael, as heads of their fathers' houses, mighty men of valor — seventeen thousand two hundred, going out for war.
And Shuppim and Huppim were the sons of Ir; and Hushim, the son of Aher.
The sons of Naphtali: Jahziel, and Guni, and Jezer, and Shallum, the sons of Bilhah.
verses.note: HEBREW: The note that the sons of Naphtali are "sons of Bilhah" (ben-Bilhah) recalls the patriarchal narrative (Gen 30:7-8; 35:25), where Bilhah, Rachel's handmaid, bore Dan and Naphtali to Jacob. The Chronicler's inclusion of this matrilineal reference is unusual and serves to validate the full tribal standing of Naphtali within the twelve-tribe structure despite irregular birth circumstances.

verses.flow: The sons of Manasseh: Asriel, whom she bore — his Aramean concubine bore Machir the father of Gilead.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The reference to Manasseh's "Aramean concubine" (pilegesh ha-aramit, Strong's H6370) reflects the historical reality of Israelite tribal intermarriage with Aramean peoples in the Transjordanian region. Machir's role as "father of Gilead" indicates he was the eponymous ancestor of the Gilead region east of the Jordan, a land allotted to half-Manasseh (Num 32:39-40).

verses.flow: And Machir took a wife for Huppim and for Shuppim, whose sister's name was Maacah — and the name of the second was Zelophehad; and Zelophehad had daughters.

verses.flow: And Maacah the wife of Machir bore a son, and she called his name Peresh; and the name of his brother was Sheresh; and his sons were Ulam and Rakem.
And the son of Ulam: Bedan. These were the sons of Gilead the son of Machir, the son of Manasseh.
And his sister Hammoleketh bore Ishhod, and Abiezer, and Mahlah.

verses.flow: And the sons of Shemida were Ahian, and Shechem, and Likhi, and Aniam.
And the sons of Ephraim: Shuthelah, and Bered his son, and Tahath his son, and Eleadah his son, and Tahath his son,
verses.note: STRUCTURE: Verses 20-27 contain a unique linear descent list for Ephraim — naming individual ancestors in succession rather than branching clans — culminating in Joshua (Yehoshua, Strong's H3091). This structural choice gives theological weight to the lineage, as Joshua is the great deliverer-figure who led Israel into the land, foreshadowing the greater Joshua (Jesus) who leads his people into eschatological rest (Heb 4:8).

verses.flow: and Zabad his son, and Shuthelah his son, and Ezer and Elead — whom the men of Gath who were born in that land killed, because they came down to take their livestock.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The raid by men of Gath on Ephraimite livestock represents an unusual pre-conquest narrative with no direct parallel elsewhere in the canonical text. Some scholars connect this to a pre-Exodus migration tradition. The loss of sons in this cattle raid prefigures the pattern of Israel's vulnerability when outside the land of promise, and sets up the birth of Beriah as a story of mourning turned toward hope.

verses.flow: And Ephraim their father mourned many days, and his brothers came to comfort him.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Ephraim's extended mourning and the coming of his brothers to "comfort" (nacham, Strong's H5162) him parallels the mourning and consolation language of the prophets (Isa 40:1; 61:2). The scene subtly anticipates the exile and restoration pattern that frames all of Chronicles: grief over loss is followed by a new beginning, pointing ultimately to messianic restoration.

verses.flow: And he went in to his wife, and she conceived and bore a son; and he called his name Beriah, because it had gone evil with his household.
verses.note: HEBREW: The name "Beriah" (Beriʿah, Strong's H1283) is explained by a folk etymology in the text itself: "because it had gone evil (bera'ah) with his household." This is a wordplay on the root r-ʿ-h (evil, calamity). Such naming-from-sorrow echoes other biblical tragedy-births (e.g., Ben-Oni in Gen 35:18), establishing a pattern where God brings forth life and lineage even through grief.

verses.flow: And his daughter was Sheerah, who built Lower and Upper Beth-horon, and Uzzen-sheerah.
And Rephah was his son, and Resheph, and Telah his son, and Tahan his son,
Ladan his son, Ammihud his son, Elishama his son,
Non his son, Joshua his son.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The culmination of the Ephraimite lineage in "Joshua his son" (Yehoshua beno) is theologically charged. Joshua (Yehoshua, "YHWH saves," Strong's H3091) shares his name with Jesus (Yeshua), and his role as the one who brought Israel into the promised land is consistently treated in the NT as a type of Christ who brings the redeemed into the true rest (Heb 4:8; Acts 7:45). The Chronicler's tracing of this lineage underscores the providential hand of YHWH through tribal history.

verses.flow: And their possession and their settlements were Bethel and its villages, and to the east Naaran, and to the west Gezer and its villages, and Shechem and its villages, and Ayyah and its villages;
and alongside the sons of Manasseh, Beth-shean and its villages, Taanach and its villages, Megiddo and its villages, Dor and its villages. In these dwelt the sons of Joseph the son of Israel.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The Chronicler closes the Joseph tribes' territorial summary by identifying their inheritance as belonging to "the sons of Joseph the son of Israel," invoking the patriarchal name Israel rather than Jacob. This is a deliberate covenantal designation, anchoring the tribal territories to the promise-name given at Peniel (Gen 32:28) and affirming that the land distribution is the fulfillment of YHWH's covenant sworn to the patriarchs.

verses.flow: The sons of Asher: Imnah, and Ishvah, and Ishvi, and Beriah, and Serah their sister.
And the sons of Beriah: Heber, and Malchiel, who was the father of Birzaith.
And Heber fathered Japhlet, and Shomer, and Hotham, and Shua their sister.
And the sons of Japhlet: Pasach, and Bimhal, and Ashvath — these are the sons of Japhlet.
And the sons of Shomer: Ahi, and Rohgah, Jehubbah, and Aram.
And the sons of his brother Helem: Zophah, and Imna, and Shelesh, and Amal.
The sons of Zophah: Suah, and Harnepher, and Shual, and Beri, and Imrah,
Bezer, and Hod, and Shamma, and Shilshah, and Ithran, and Beera.
And the sons of Jether: Jephunneh, and Pispa, and Ara.
And the sons of Ulla: Arah, and Hanniel, and Rizia.

verses.flow: All these were the sons of Asher, heads of fathers' houses, select mighty men of valor, chief of the princes — and their enrollment by genealogy for service in war was twenty-six thousand men.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The closing summary of Asher's genealogy uses the full honorific formula: "heads of fathers' houses, select mighty men of valor, chief of the princes." The Hebrew word for "select" or "choice" (berurim, from barar, Strong's H1305) carries the sense of those who have been tested and proved. The enrollment figure of 26,000 completes the military census pattern running through the chapter, which collectively portrays all Israel as a covenant army — a kingdom of priests and warriors standing before YHWH.

verses.flow: And Benjamin fathered Bela his firstborn, Ashbel the second, Aharah the third,
verses.note: HEBREW: The chapter opens the Benjaminite genealogy with "וּבִנְיָמִין הוֹלִיד" (u-Vinyamin holid), "and Benjamin fathered," using the standard toledoth formula. This list partially overlaps with but diverges from the genealogy in Gen 46:21 and Num 26:38-41, reflecting variant tribal traditions preserved in the Chronicler's sources. The name Bela (בֶּלַע, bela') means "swallowing" or "devouring."

verses.flow: Nohah the fourth, and Rapha the fifth.

verses.flow: These are the sons of Ehud — these were the heads of fathers' houses of the inhabitants of Geba, and they were carried into exile to Manahath:
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The exile to Manahath mentioned here is an internal tribal relocation, not the Babylonian exile. Manahath (מָנַחַת) was a Calebite settlement in Judah (cf. 1 Chr 2:52, 54), suggesting early inter-tribal conflict and population transfer within the land. The reference to Geba as a Benjaminite city is consistent with Josh 21:17.

verses.flow: Naaman, and Ahijah, and Gera — he carried them into exile — and he fathered Uzza and Ahihud.
And Shaharaim fathered sons in the field of Moab after he had sent away Hushim and Baara his wives.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Shaharaim's settlement in Moab and his sending away of multiple wives reflects polygynous practice and cross-cultural ANE marriage customs. The phrase "field of Moab" (שְׂדֵה מוֹאָב, sedeh Mo'av) echoes Ruth 1:1-2, pointing to a region with known Israelite connections. Such genealogical notices of Transjordanian settlement illuminate later settlement patterns.

verses.flow: And he fathered from Hodesh his wife: Jobab, and Zibia, and Mesha, and Malcam,
and Jeuz, and Sachia, and Mirmah. These are his sons, heads of fathers' houses.
And from Hushim he fathered Abitub and Elpaal.
And the sons of Elpaal: Eber, and Misham, and Shemed — who built Ono and Lod with its daughter-towns —
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The construction note regarding Ono and Lod (Lydda) embedded within a genealogy is characteristic of Chronicler's style, connecting family lineage to land occupation and civic building. Lod (לֹד) later appears in Ezra 2:33 and Neh 7:37 as a post-exilic resettlement site, making this genealogical note of practical importance for returned exiles establishing land claims.

verses.flow: and Beriah, and Shema — these were heads of fathers' houses of the inhabitants of Aijalon, who drove out the inhabitants of Gath —
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Aijalon (אַיָּלוֹן) was a city in the Shephelah associated with Joshua's famous battle (Josh 10:12). The note that these Benjaminites "drove out the inhabitants of Gath" suggests a period of Benjaminite military expansion westward into Philistine territory, though the precise historical context is uncertain. Such martial notices within genealogies assert territorial rights.

verses.flow: and Ahio, Shashak, and Jeremoth,
and Zebadiah, and Arad, and Eder,

and Jaareshiah, and Elijah, and Zichri were sons of Jeroham.
These were the heads of fathers' houses, by their generations, chiefs — these dwelt in Jerusalem.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "these dwelt in Jerusalem" (הֵמָּה יָשְׁבוּ בִירוּשָׁלִָם) is theologically significant in the post-exilic context of Chronicles. The Chronicler is establishing the legitimacy of Benjaminite presence in the holy city, demonstrating continuity of covenant community across the exile. Jerusalem as the dwelling place of God's people is a recurring covenantal theme in Chronicles.

verses.flow: And in Gibeon dwelt the father of Gibeon — and the name of his wife was Maacah —
verses.note: STRUCTURE: Verses 29-38 form a Gibeonite-Saulide genealogy that is repeated nearly verbatim in 1 Chr 9:35-44. This deliberate repetition serves as a literary bridge, connecting the all-Israel genealogies of chapters 1-9 with the narrative of Saul's reign and death in chapter 10. The Chronicler frames Saul as a foil for David, whose dynasty is the true vehicle of covenant promise.

verses.flow: and his firstborn son was Abdon, then Zur, and Kish, and Baal, and Nadab,
and Gedor, and Ahio, and Zecher.
And Mikloth fathered Shimeah. And these also, alongside their brothers, dwelt in Jerusalem opposite their brothers.
And Ner fathered Kish, and Kish fathered Saul, and Saul fathered Jonathan, and Malchishua, and Abinadab, and Eshbaal.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The lineage "Ner fathered Kish, Kish fathered Saul" establishes the royal house of Benjamin. The name Eshbaal (אֶשְׁבַּעַל, esh-ba'al, "man of Baal") for Saul's son is the original form, elsewhere replaced by Ish-bosheth ("man of shame") in Samuel, a later scribal substitution of "bosheth" (shame) for "Baal" to avoid honoring the Canaanite deity. The Chronicler preserves the older form.

verses.flow: And the son of Jonathan was Merib-baal, and Merib-baal fathered Micah.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Merib-baal (מְרִיב-בַּעַל, "Baal contends" or "he who contends with Baal") is the original name for the figure called Mephibosheth in 2 Sam 4:4; 9:6, again reflecting the scribal substitution of "bosheth" for "Baal." The Chronicler's retention of the Baal-element in the name is not an endorsement of Baal worship but a faithful use of archival records, demonstrating his commitment to textual preservation.

verses.flow: And the sons of Micah: Pithon, and Melech, and Tarea, and Ahaz.
And Ahaz fathered Jehoaddah, and Jehoaddah fathered Alemeth, and Azmaveth, and Zimri, and Zimri fathered Moza.
And Moza fathered Binea; Raphah was his son, Eleasah his son, Azel his son.
And Azel had six sons, and these are their names: Azrikam, Bocheru, and Ishmael, and Sheariah, and Obadiah, and Hanan. All these were sons of Azel.
And the sons of Eshek his brother: Ulam his firstborn, Jeush the second, and Eliphelet the third.

verses.flow: And the sons of Ulam were mighty men of valor, archers, and had many sons and grandsons — a hundred and fifty. All these were from the sons of Benjamin.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The closing note that Ulam's descendants were "mighty men of valor (גִּבּוֹרֵי חַיִל, gibborey chayil), archers" echoes the martial language applied to David's mighty men (1 Chr 11-12). The specific mention of 150 descendants underscores the vitality and blessing of the tribe even after the failures of the Saulide dynasty. The tribe of Benjamin's enduring strength points to God's faithfulness to covenant community.

verses.flow: So all Israel was enrolled by genealogies (yachas), and behold, they are written in the Book of the Kings of Israel. And Judah was carried away captive to Babylon because of their unfaithfulness (ma'al).
verses.note: HEBREW: "yachas" (H3187, from yachas, to enroll by genealogy) is the thematic word of the opening chapters of Chronicles, emphasizing covenantal lineage and identity. "Ma'al" (H4603, unfaithfulness/treachery) is a priestly-legal term for covenant breach; its use here frames the exile as a liturgical transgression, not merely a political failure. This verse serves as both summary and hinge, closing the genealogical prelude and opening the restoration narrative.

verses.flow: Now the first to dwell again in their possessions in their cities were Israel, the priests, the Levites, and the temple servants (Nethinim).
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Nethinim" (H5411, from natan, given/dedicated) refers to temple servants, likely descendants of the Gibeonites pressed into sanctuary labor (Josh 9:27). Their prominence in the return list underscores that the restored community was defined by temple service, not merely ethnic descent. This verse likely reflects the early Persian period returnees under Zerubbabel (Ezra 2; Neh 7).

verses.flow: And of the Levites: Shemaiah the son of Hasshub, son of Azrikam, son of Hashabiah, of the sons of Merari;
and Bakbakkar, Heresh, Galal, and Mattaniah the son of Mica, son of Zichri, son of Asaph;
and Obadiah the son of Shemaiah, son of Galal, son of Jeduthun; and Berechiah the son of Asa, son of Elkanah, who dwelt in the villages of the Netophathites.
And the gatekeepers (sho'arim): Shallum, Akkub, Talmon, Ahiman; and their kinsman Shallum was the chief.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Sho'arim" (H7778, gatekeepers, from sha'ar, gate) denotes an important Levitical office with deep covenantal significance. The gate in ancient Israelite thought was a liminal, sacred threshold — the place of judgment, covenant transaction, and divine protection. That the chief gatekeeper is named first signals the theological weight of this role as guardian of holy space.

verses.flow: Until now he has been in the King's Gate on the east side. These were the gatekeepers for the camps of the sons of Levi.

And Shallum the son of Kore, son of Ebiasaph, son of Korah, and his kinsmen of his father's house, the Korahites, were over the work of the service, keepers of the thresholds of the tent; and their fathers had been over the camp of YHWH, keepers of the entrance.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The phrase "camp of YHWH" (machaneh YHWH) evokes the wilderness tradition of the divine warrior encampment (Num 2), where YHWH dwelt in the midst of Israel's ordered tribal assembly. The Korahite gatekeepers stand in continuity with those who guarded the sacred threshold of the tabernacle, the dwelling of the divine presence. This sacred guardianship reflects the broader cosmic pattern of divine throne-guardians present throughout the ANE and in Hebrew heavenly council imagery.

verses.flow: And Phinehas the son of Eleazar was the chief officer over them in former times; YHWH was with him.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The commendation "YHWH was with him" placed on Phinehas recalls the covenant of a perpetual priesthood granted to him for his zeal (Num 25:10-13). The Chronicler deliberately grounds the post-exilic temple order in this ancient covenantal validation, demonstrating that the restoration priesthood carries legitimate divine sanction.

verses.flow: Zechariah the son of Meshelemiah was gatekeeper at the entrance of the tent of meeting.

All these, who were chosen as gatekeepers of the thresholds, were two hundred and twelve. They were enrolled by genealogies in their villages. David and Samuel the seer had established them in their office of trust.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The pairing of David and Samuel as joint founders of the gatekeeping order is striking. Samuel is here called "the seer (ha-ro'eh)" — his earlier role as prophet-seer (1 Sam 9:9) is invoked to give prophetic authority to the Davidic temple arrangements. The Chronicler presents the entire cultic order as prophetically ordained, not merely administratively convenient. (1 Sam 9:9; 1 Chr 26:1-19)

verses.flow: So they and their sons were in charge of the gates of the house of YHWH, that is, the house of the tent, as guards.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "office of trust" (emunah, H530) placed upon the four chief gatekeepers evokes covenantal faithfulness — the same root as "amen." This is not mere institutional reliability but a theological category: these men bear the character of YHWH himself who is faithful (emun). The treasury and chamber oversight reflects the same pattern seen in the Second Temple period (Neh 13:13).

verses.flow: And Mattithiah, one of the Levites, the firstborn of Shallum the Korahite, was in trusted charge over the things baked in pans.
And some of their kinsmen of the sons of the Kohathites had charge of the bread of the Presence (lechem happanim), preparing it every Sabbath.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The "lechem happanim" (bread of the Presence, literally "bread of the face/faces") placed before YHWH each Sabbath (Lev 24:5-9) is a perpetual covenant sign of YHWH's provision and Israel's communion with him. Jesus' declaration that he is the "bread of life" (John 6:35) and his institution of the Lord's Supper stands in direct typological continuity with this weekly renewal of covenant at YHWH's table.

verses.flow: Now these, the singers (meshorerim), heads of fathers' houses of the Levites, were in the chambers, free from other service, for they were on duty day and night.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Meshorerim" (H7891, singers, from shir, to sing) in the Levitical context denotes not mere performers but liturgical mediators whose music was a form of prophetic ministry before YHWH (1 Chr 25:1-3). The note that they were "free from other service" reflects the same theological principle as the Levitical tithe — some are wholly set apart for the work of presence and praise.
